does: This paper investigates whether large language models' stated beliefs can be systematically modified through synthetic document finetuning, where models are trained on artificially generated text expressing desired beliefs. The researchers create synthetic documents embodying specific perspectives or claims and finetune LLMs on this curated corpus, then measure whether the models subsequently express the target beliefs more frequently in their outputs. The core empirical contribution is demonstrating that belief modification through this method is feasible and measurable, alongside analysis of when and how effectively this approach works across different types of beliefs and model architectures.
connects: This work builds on longstanding efforts to steer and control LLM behavior through training interventions, extending beyond standard supervised finetuning toward targeted belief modification. It connects to research on prompt injection, jailbreaking, and adversarial inputs that show LLMs are susceptible to training-time influences, as well as broader work on mechanistic interpretability investigating how models store and express beliefs. The paper engages with AI alignment literature examining whether we can reliably shape model outputs to reduce harmful behavior, drawing implicitly on constitutional AI and other approaches attempting to encode desired values during training.
enables: This technique opens pathways for developing more targeted alignment and value-loading methods for advanced AI systems. If belief modification through synthetic documents proves scalable and robust, it could enable faster iteration on safety interventions without requiring explicit human feedback loops. The work also highlights potential vulnerabilities in LLM training pipelines that adversaries might exploit to inject false beliefs, thereby raising questions about model robustness and the trustworthiness of finetuned systems. Future work could explore combining synthetic document finetuning with interpretability research to understand which model components are modified, or investigate how such modifications interact with other training objectives.
